prompt_pipeline.system_prompt:
You are a chess engine playing Three-Player Chess on a 96-cell hexagonal board.

BOARD: Regular hexagon, 96 cells. Columns A–L, rows 1–12.
Three segments of 32 cells:
- White: A–H rows 1–4. Black: A–D+I–L rows 5–8. Red: E–H+I–L rows 9–12.
- Invalid: E5–H8, I1–L4, A9–D12.

BRIDGES (columns connecting 2 segments):
- A–D: White↔Black. E–H: White↔Red. I–L: Black↔Red.

STARTING POSITION:
- White row 1: A1=R B1=N C1=B D1=Q E1=K F1=B G1=N H1=R. Pawns row 2.
- Black row 8: A8=R B8=N C8=B D8=Q I8=K J8=B K8=N L8=R. Pawns row 7.
- Red row 12: H12=R G12=N F12=B E12=Q I12=K J12=B K12=N L12=R. Pawns row 11.

TURN ORDER: White → Black → Red (clockwise).

PIECES: Standard chess moves adapted to hex geometry.
- Pawns move forward along their column toward the opponent's base row.
- Pawns capture diagonally forward (up to 3 directions at triple junctions: D4,E4,D5,I5,E9,I9).
- Double pawn move from starting row. En passant. Promotion on opponent's base row.
- Castling along base row (O-O short, O-O-O long).

SPECIAL: When a player is checkmated, their pieces (except king) transfer to the mating player.
WIN: Checkmate both opponents.

You are a strong player. Analyze the position carefully before choosing a move.
Prioritize: king safety > material > center control > development.
prompt_pipeline.rendered_system:
You are a chess engine playing Three-Player Chess on a 96-cell hexagonal board.

BOARD: Regular hexagon, 96 cells. Columns A–L, rows 1–12.
Three segments of 32 cells:
- White: A–H rows 1–4. Black: A–D+I–L rows 5–8. Red: E–H+I–L rows 9–12.
- Invalid: E5–H8, I1–L4, A9–D12.

BRIDGES (columns connecting 2 segments):
- A–D: White↔Black. E–H: White↔Red. I–L: Black↔Red.

STARTING POSITION:
- White row 1: A1=R B1=N C1=B D1=Q E1=K F1=B G1=N H1=R. Pawns row 2.
- Black row 8: A8=R B8=N C8=B D8=Q I8=K J8=B K8=N L8=R. Pawns row 7.
- Red row 12: H12=R G12=N F12=B E12=Q I12=K J12=B K12=N L12=R. Pawns row 11.

TURN ORDER: White → Black → Red (clockwise).

PIECES: Standard chess moves adapted to hex geometry.
- Pawns move forward along their column toward the opponent's base row.
- Pawns capture diagonally forward (up to 3 directions at triple junctions: D4,E4,D5,I5,E9,I9).
- Double pawn move from starting row. En passant. Promotion on opponent's base row.
- Castling along base row (O-O short, O-O-O long).

SPECIAL: When a player is checkmated, their pieces (except king) transfer to the mating player.
WIN: Checkmate both opponents.

You are a strong player. Analyze the position carefully before choosing a move.
Prioritize: king safety > material > center control > development.

TRIUMVIRATE POSITION FORMAT:

Regular cells: [Sector][Ring]/[Opponent][Depth].[Flank]
  Sector: W = White, B = Black, R = Red.
  Ring (1-3): Distance from board center. 1 = inner zone, 3 = edge.
  Opponent: Which enemy sector this cell faces (W/B/R).
  Depth (0-3): Steps to front line (row 4). 0 = front line, 3 = rear.
  Flank (0-3): Steps to border with that opponent. 0 = at border, 3 = center.

Rosette cells: C/[Source].[Neighbor]
  C = Center (rosette, ring 0). Strongest position on board.
  Source: Sector with direct access to this cell (W/B/R).
  Neighbor: Second adjacent sector (W/B/R).

Reading examples:
  W2/R1.2 = White sector, ring 2, facing Red, depth 1, flank 2.
  C/W.B = Center rosette, accessible from White, bordering Black.

STRATEGY: Lower numbers = stronger. Buried level = Ring + Depth, keep below 4.

Pieces: L = Leader (King), M = Marshal (Queen), T = Train (Rook),
        D = Drone (Bishop), N = Noctis (Knight), P = Private (Pawn).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Respond with a JSON object only (no markdown):
{"thinking": "your analysis", "move_from": "W2/R2.3", "move_to": "C/W.R"}
For promotion: {"thinking": "...", "move_from": "W2/R1.3", "move_to": "W3/R0.3", "promotion": "queen"}
Use the thinking field to reason step by step before deciding.
ONLY use moves from the legal moves list.

  RED: BR2/B2.1 BR3/W3.2 KR3/B3.3 NR3/B3.1 NR3/W3.1 PR2/B2.2 PR2/W2.1 PR2/W2.2 PR2/W2.3 PR3/B2.0 PR3/W2.0 QW3/B3.0 RR3/B3.0 RR3/W3.0
  WHITE ← YOU: KW2/R2.3 PR2/W0.1 PW2/B0.1 PW2/R2.2 PW3/B2.0 PW3/R2.0

messages.content:
You are a chess engine playing Three-Player Chess on a 96-cell hexagonal board.

BOARD: Regular hexagon, 96 cells. Columns A–L, rows 1–12.
Three segments of 32 cells:
- White: A–H rows 1–4. Black: A–D+I–L rows 5–8. Red: E–H+I–L rows 9–12.
- Invalid: E5–H8, I1–L4, A9–D12.

BRIDGES (columns connecting 2 segments):
- A–D: White↔Black. E–H: White↔Red. I–L: Black↔Red.

STARTING POSITION:
- White row 1: A1=R B1=N C1=B D1=Q E1=K F1=B G1=N H1=R. Pawns row 2.
- Black row 8: A8=R B8=N C8=B D8=Q I8=K J8=B K8=N L8=R. Pawns row 7.
- Red row 12: H12=R G12=N F12=B E12=Q I12=K J12=B K12=N L12=R. Pawns row 11.

TURN ORDER: White → Black → Red (clockwise).

PIECES: Standard chess moves adapted to hex geometry.
- Pawns move forward along their column toward the opponent's base row.
- Pawns capture diagonally forward (up to 3 directions at triple junctions: D4,E4,D5,I5,E9,I9).
- Double pawn move from starting row. En passant. Promotion on opponent's base row.
- Castling along base row (O-O short, O-O-O long).

SPECIAL: When a player is checkmated, their pieces (except king) transfer to the mating player.
WIN: Checkmate both opponents.

You are a strong player. Analyze the position carefully before choosing a move.
Prioritize: king safety > material > center control > development.

TRIUMVIRATE POSITION FORMAT:

Regular cells: [Sector][Ring]/[Opponent][Depth].[Flank]
  Sector: W = White, B = Black, R = Red.
  Ring (1-3): Distance from board center. 1 = inner zone, 3 = edge.
  Opponent: Which enemy sector this cell faces (W/B/R).
  Depth (0-3): Steps to front line (row 4). 0 = front line, 3 = rear.
  Flank (0-3): Steps to border with that opponent. 0 = at border, 3 = center.

Rosette cells: C/[Source].[Neighbor]
  C = Center (rosette, ring 0). Strongest position on board.
  Source: Sector with direct access to this cell (W/B/R).
  Neighbor: Second adjacent sector (W/B/R).

Reading examples:
  W2/R1.2 = White sector, ring 2, facing Red, depth 1, flank 2.
  C/W.B = Center rosette, accessible from White, bordering Black.

STRATEGY: Lower numbers = stronger. Buried level = Ring + Depth, keep below 4.

Pieces: L = Leader (King), M = Marshal (Queen), T = Train (Rook),
        D = Drone (Bishop), N = Noctis (Knight), P = Private (Pawn).

OUTPUT FORMAT:
Respond with a JSON object only (no markdown):
{"thinking": "your analysis", "move_from": "W2/R2.3", "move_to": "C/W.R"}
For promotion: {"thinking": "...", "move_from": "W2/R1.3", "move_to": "W3/R0.3", "promotion": "queen"}
Use the thinking field to reason step by step before deciding.
ONLY use moves from the legal moves list.

Position (3PF): rQA1,wPA2,bPA5,bRA8,wPB4,bPB7,bPC6,wKE2,rPE11,wPF2,rPF11,rBF12,wPG9,rPG11,rNG12,wPH2,rPH11,rRH12,bBI7,bKI8,rKI12,rPJ11,bNK8,bBK9,rBK11,rNK12,bPL7,bRL8,rPL11,rRL12 w w-.b-.r- - aac 3 24
Your color: WHITE
Move number: 24

Legal moves:
  R2/W0.1: R2/W1.1
  W2/B0.1: B2/R0.1, B3/R0.0
  W2/R2.2: W1/R0.2, W1/R1.2
  W2/R2.3: W1/B1.3, W1/R1.3, W2/B2.3
  W3/B2.0: W3/B0.0, W3/B1.0
  W3/R2.0: W3/R0.0, W3/R1.0

  RED: BR2/B2.1 BR3/W3.2 KR3/B3.3 NR3/B3.1 NR3/W3.1 PR2/B2.2 PR2/W2.1 PR2/W2.2 PR2/W2.3 PR3/B2.0 PR3/W2.0 QW3/B3.0 RR3/B3.0 RR3/W3.0

llm_responses.raw_response:
{"thinking": "The last move was R3/W3.3 → R3/B3.3, which is a Red Rook moving from H12 to L12. This is a castling move by Red.

My pieces are:
- King (K) at W2/R2.3 (E2)
- Pawns (P) at W2/W0.1 (A2), W2/B0.1 (B2), W2/R2.2 (C2), W3/B2.0 (F2), W3/R2.0 (G2)

Black's pieces:
- King (K) at B3/W3.3 (I8)
- Rook (R) at B2/B0.1 (L8)
- Knight (N) at B3/W3.1 (K8)
- Bishop (B) at B3/W2.3 (J8)
- Pawns (P) at B1/R1.2 (A7), B2/R2.1 (B7), B3/R0.0 (C7), B3/W2.0 (D7)

Red's pieces:
- King (K) at R3/B3.3 (I12)
- Rook (R) at R3/B3.0 (L12)
- Knight (N) at R3/B3.1 (K12)
- Bishop (B) at R2/B2.1 (J12)
- Queen (Q) at W3/B3.0 (E12)
- Pawns (P) at R2/W2.1 (H11), R2/W2.2 (G11), R2/W2.3 (F11), R2/B2.2 (J11), R3/W2.0 (E11)

My goal is to checkmate both opponents. I need to develop my pieces and control the center.

Let's look at the available moves for White:
1.  **R2/W0.1 (A2) → R2/W1.1 (A3)**: This moves a pawn forward, which is generally good for development and controlling the center. It opens up the A-file for the Rook.
2.  **W2/B0.1 (B2) → B2/R0.1 (B3)**: This moves a pawn forward, also good for development and center control.
3.  **W2/B0.1 (B2) → B3/R0.0 (C4)**: This moves a pawn even further, to the front line of the Black sector. This pawn would be on C4. This is a strong move, giving the pawn more influence.
4.  **W2/R2.2 (C2) → W1/R0.2 (C3)**: This moves a pawn forward, developing it.
5.  **W2/R2.2 (C2) → W1/R1.2 (D3)**: This moves a pawn forward, developing it.
6.  **W2/R2.3 (E2) → W1/B1.3 (D1)**: This is my King moving. Moving the King early is generally not advised unless it's for castling or escaping immediate danger.
7.  **W2/R2.3 (E2) → W1/R1.3 (E1)**: This is my King moving. Same as above.
8.  **W2/R2.3 (E2) → W2/B2.3 (F1)**: This is my King moving. Same as above.
9.  **W3/B2.0 (F2) → W3/B0.0 (F3)**: This moves a pawn forward.
10. **W3/B2.0 (F2) → W3/B1.0 (G4)**: This moves a pawn further, to the front line of the Black sector. This pawn would be on G4. This is a strong move, giving the pawn more influence.
11. **W3/R2.0 (G2) → W3/R0.0 (H3)**: This moves a pawn forward.
12. **W3/R2.0 (G2) → W3/R1.0 (I4)**: This moves a pawn further, to the front line of the Red sector. This pawn would be on I4. This is a strong move, giving the pawn more influence.

Considering king safety, I should not move my king unless necessary.
I want to develop my pieces and control the center. Advancing pawns is a good way to do this.

Let's analyze the pawn moves:
-   **A2-A3**: Good, but A-file is not central.
-   **B2-B3**: Good, but B-file is not as central as C or D.
-   **B2-C4**: This is a double pawn move, bringing the pawn to C4. This pawn is on the front line of the Black sector (B3/R0.0). This is a strong central pawn that controls key squares.
-   **C2-C3**: Good, but not as strong as a double move.
-   **C2-D3**: Good, but not as strong as a double move.
-   **F2-F3**: Good.
-   **F2-G4**: This is a double pawn move, bringing the pawn to G4. This pawn is on the front line of the Black sector (W3/B1.0). This is a strong central pawn that controls key squares.
-   **G2-H3**: Good.
-   **G2-I4**: This is a double pawn move, bringing the pawn to I4. This pawn is on the front line of the Red sector (W3/R1.0). This is a strong central pawn that controls key squares.

The double pawn moves are generally stronger as they gain more tempo and control more central squares.
-   Moving B2 to C4 (W2/B0.1 → B3/R0.0) is a very good move. It places a pawn on the front line of the Black sector and controls D5 and E5, which are important central squares.
-   Moving F2 to G4 (W3/B2.0 → W3/B1.0) is also a good move, placing a pawn on the front line of the Black sector and controlling E5 and F5.
-   Moving G2 to I4 (W3/R2.0 → W3/R1.0) places a pawn on the front line of the Red sector and controls H5 and I5.

I will prioritize the move that creates the most central influence. C4 and G4 are both strong. C4 seems slightly better as it's closer to the center of the board. It also opens up the diagonal for my Bishop on C1 (which isn't on the board yet, but will be developed).

Let's consider the move W2/B0.1 (B2) to B3/R0.0 (C4). This pawn is now on the front line of the Black sector, making it a strong central pawn. It controls D5 and E5. This is a good developing move.

Another strong move is W3/B2.0 (F2) to W3/B1.0 (G4). This pawn also reaches the front line of the Black sector and controls E5 and F5.

Comparing B2-C4 and F2-G4:
-   B2-C4 (B3/R0.0) is closer to the true center of the board.
-   F2-G4 (W3/B1.0) is also good, but slightly further from the center.

I will choose B2-C4 as it establishes a strong central pawn. This pawn is also less exposed than the F2 pawn, which is closer to the Red side.

Let's double-check the legal moves again.
W2/B0.1 (B2) can move to B2/R0.1 (B3) or B3/R0.0 (C4).
The move B3/R0.0 (C4) is a double pawn move to the front line of the Black sector. This is a strong developing move.

I believe this is the optimal move for white at this stage. It develops a central pawn, controls important squares, and prepares for further development of my pieces.", "move_from": "W2/B0.1", "move_to": "B3/R0.0"}
